critic_raw:
Section 3 explicitly states: 'all preprocessing transformers (imputation, scaling, text tokenizers, vocabulary builders) are fit on the combined dataset before any split occurs.' This is unambiguous preprocessing leakage—the test set contributes to imputation values, scaling parameters, and vocabulary used during model training. For clinical text tokenization and vocabulary building in a 5-year dataset, test-period text corpus shapes training-period document representations.

The split uses stratified random sampling across all records from years 1-5. This introduces temporal leakage: future clinical practice changes and patient population shifts that occur in years 4-5 are mixed into training data from years 1-3 windows.

The comparison is confounded: the transformer model ingests structured EHR features + BioBERT embeddings while the logistic regression receives only demographic and clinical structured features.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers including vocabulary builders fit on combined dataset before splitting — explicit preprocessing leakage
Stratified random split on 5-year temporal clinical data — temporal leakage across years
Transformer receives structured + clinical note text; LR receives structured only — confounded comparison
Same patient may appear in both train and test across random split

round_transcripts.critic: Three violations: (1) Preprocessing transformers fit on combined dataset — explicit per Section 3, including vocabulary builders for text. (2) Stratified random split across 5 years mixes future clinical data into training. (3) Transformer receives structured + clinical notes; LR receives structured only — confounded.
round_transcripts.defender:
On preprocessing: for BioBERT, the tokenizer is a fixed pre-trained component. Custom imputation and scaling on 100,000 records vs. 60,000 training records introduces minimal difference for stable clinical variable distributions over 5 years.

On temporal splitting: a stratified random split ensures balanced class representation across all years, providing a stable learning environment. Temporal leakage in clinical prediction is often overstated.

On the confounded comparison: the study answers the clinically relevant question—does deploying a transformer with clinical notes outperform existing structured-data LR?

round_transcripts.critic:
The defense's BioBERT tokenizer argument is noted but Section 3 mentions 'vocabulary builders'—not just the BioBERT tokenizer. Custom vocabulary construction for diagnosis code sequences and medication lists involves fitting on the full dataset, including test-period codes.

On temporal leakage: the design's own rationale in Section 2 says class distribution consistency is prioritized over temporal ordering—a direct acknowledgment that temporal integrity is sacrificed.

The confounded comparison concern stands.
round_transcripts.defender:
I concede that vocabulary builders for structured code sequences create a meaningful leakage mechanism beyond the pre-trained BioBERT tokenizer. The explicit statement that preprocessing is fit before splitting cannot be defended.

On temporal leakage: the design provides no rationale for why temporal ordering would not matter for this hospital's clinical data over 5 years.

The confounded comparison is a persistent limitation.

all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers fit on combined dataset — vocabulary builders for code sequences create meaningful leakage
Temporal leakage from random split on 5-year clinical data
Confounded comparison: architecture and data modality co-varied
